question: Which SOLID principle is violated by passing a string identifier for an algorithm instead of a strategy object?

options.text: Open/Closed Principle — adding a new algorithm requires changing existing code (adding a new if/else)
Single Responsibility Principle — the function does too much
options.explanation: SRP concerns the number of reasons for a class to change. The problem here is extensibility — code must change when adding a new algorithm

options.text: Liskov Substitution Principle — strings are not interchangeable
options.explanation: LSP concerns subtypes and inheritance. String identifiers violate OCP, not LSP

options.text: Interface Segregation Principle — the interface is too wide
options.explanation: ISP concerns interface splitting. The problem with a string identifier is the inability to extend without modifying code

question: Why is it important to provide an unsubscribe method (off/unsubscribe) in the Observer pattern?

options.text: Without unsubscribing, handlers remain in memory causing leaks — especially critical in React with useEffect
So TypeScript doesn't show warnings about unused variables
options.explanation: TypeScript doesn't link subscriptions to variables. Unsubscribing is needed for memory management and lifecycle

options.text: Because without off you can't subscribe again
options.explanation: Resubscribing is possible without off — the problem is specifically memory leaks from unremoved handlers

options.text: It's a requirement of the EventEmitter interface in Node.js
options.explanation: We're creating our own typed EventEmitter. Unsubscribing is an architectural necessity, not a Node.js requirement

question: What must each Command save for undo to work correctly?

options.text: A snapshot of the previous state so changes can be rolled back
A reference to the next command in the chain
options.explanation: References to other commands are not the Command's responsibility. Each command must be self-sufficient for undo

options.text: The command execution time for logging
options.explanation: Execution time is useful for logs but not required for undo. Undo needs a state snapshot

options.text: A reference to the user who initiated the command
options.explanation: User information may be useful for auditing, but undo specifically needs a data snapshot

question: What advantages does a typed EventEmitter with an event map provide?

options.text: TypeScript verifies that emit data matches the event type
Auto-completion of event names in the IDE
Cannot subscribe to a non-existent event
Automatic removal of listeners when a component is destroyed
options.explanation: Typing doesn't manage subscription lifecycle — unsubscribing must be implemented manually

options.text: Guaranteed order of handler invocation
options.explanation: Typing doesn't determine invocation order — that's the implementation's job, not the type system's

question: What should happen in a Chain of Responsibility if no handler processes the request?

options.text: The behavior should be defined in advance — e.g., return the request as-is or throw an error
The chain should automatically restart from the beginning
options.explanation: Restarting the chain could lead to an infinite loop. The end-of-chain behavior must be explicitly defined

options.text: The request is ignored — this is normal behavior
options.explanation: Silently ignoring a request is a common mistake. It can lead to data loss and hard-to-find bugs

options.text: TypeScript will automatically prevent this situation
options.explanation: TypeScript cannot guarantee at the type level that at least one handler will process the request — this is a system design concern
